citations: Chirinko (2008) 'σ: The Long and Short of It' - surveys literature and finds central estimate around 0.4-0.6 | Antràs (2004) 'Is the U.S. Aggregate Production Function Cobb-Douglas?' - estimates below unity, around 0.6-0.8 | Karabarbounis and Neiman (2014) 'The Global Decline of the Labor Share' - argue for elasticity above 1 to explain labor share trends
reasoning_summary: The literature on the aggregate capital-labor elasticity of substitution is contentious. Many time-series and cross-sectional estimates cluster below unity, often in the 0.4–0.8 range (Chirinko's survey, Antràs). However, some studies focusing on long-run variation or labor share trends (Karabarbounis & Neiman) suggest values near or above 1. The long-run elasticity is typically estimated to be higher than short-run estimates due to adjustment frictions. Balancing the bulk of evidence pointing below 1 with the possibility that long-run adjustment pushes it closer to or slightly above 1, I center my belief at 0.7 with meaningful right-tail uncertainty extending above 1.
